past_medical_history: Vitamin D deficiency, diagnosed 2 years ago
Irritable bowel syndrome, diagnosed 5 years ago
Depression, diagnosed 3 years ago, currently in remission
family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes diagnosed at age 58
No family history of multiple sclerosis or other demyelinating diseases

social_history.living_situation: Lives with her partner in a second-floor apartment
social_history.exercise: Previously active runner, 3-4 times per week; has reduced to once weekly due to leg fatigue over past 2 months

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Attention intact — performs serial sevens and months backward without error. Language is fluent without paraphasic errors. Memory: 3/3 immediate recall, 2/3 at five minutes (missed 'tulip'). No evidence of cognitive impairment on bedside screening.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3.5 mm bilaterally, reactive to light and accommodation. Visual acuity 20/25 right eye, 20/40 left eye with mild red desaturation in the left eye. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Mild relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) on the left. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus at primary gaze, but subtle bilateral horizontal gaze-evoked nystagmus present on extreme lateral gaze. No internuclear ophthalmoplegia identified. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. No facial asymmetry. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 strength throughout bilaterally. Lower extremities: left hip flexion 4+/5, left knee extension 4/5, left ankle dorsiflexion 4+/5; right lower extremity 5/5 throughout. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact in upper extremities bilaterally. Decreased vibration sense at the left great toe (perceives 4 seconds versus 12 seconds on the right). Pinprick mildly diminished below the level of the umbilicus on the left compared to the right. Temperature sensation not formally tested. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 3+ bilaterally (brisk). Achilles 2+ on the right, 3+ on the left. Left plantar response extensor (Babinski positive). Right plantar response flexor. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing: mild dysmetria on the left, intact on the right. No dysdiadochokinesia.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly spastic gait pattern with left foot circumduction. Tandem gait impaired with 3 missteps over 10 steps. Romberg sign positive with tendency to fall to the left. Unable to hop on left foot.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign positive — electric-shock sensation down the spine on neck flexion. No meningismus. Uhthoff phenomenon reported by patient: symptoms worsen after hot showers.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive bilateral leg weakness and urinary urgency over six weeks
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Sarah Kowalski is a 33-year-old right-handed Caucasian woman who presents to the neurology inpatient service after being referred urgently by her primary care physician for progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness and new-onset urinary symptoms. She reports that approximately six weeks ago, she began noticing that her left leg felt 'heavy' while climbing the stairs to her apartment, initially attributing it to fatigue from work stress. Over the following two weeks, the heaviness progressed and she noticed her left foot occasionally dragging, particularly toward the end of the day. Three weeks ago, she began experiencing urinary urgency with several episodes of near-incontinence, which she found distressing and unlike anything she had experienced before. She also reports a band-like tightness sensation around her lower abdomen that she describes as feeling like she is wearing a tight belt. On further questioning, she recalls an episode approximately eight months ago in which she developed blurred vision in her left eye lasting about ten days, accompanied by pain with eye movement. She saw her optometrist at the time who noted mild optic disc pallor and referred her to ophthalmology, but she did not follow up on the referral as the symptoms had largely resolved. She also recalls a two-week episode of tingling in her right hand and forearm approximately fourteen months ago that resolved spontaneously and which she attributed to carpal tunnel syndrome from typing at work. She denies any recent fevers, weight loss, joint pains, oral ulcers, or skin rashes. She notes that her symptoms seem to worsen noticeably after taking hot showers, with her leg weakness becoming more pronounced for 30 to 60 minutes afterward. Over the past week, her partner has noticed that she appears more fatigued than usual and has been stumbling when walking around the apartment.

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular white matter, predominantly along the lateral ventricles with several lesions oriented perpendicular to the ventricular surface
findings.size: Multiple lesions ranging from 5 mm to 15 mm

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense ovoid lesions oriented perpendicular to the ventricles
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, well-demarcated lesions in periventricular distribution
signal_characteristics.T1: Several lesions show corresponding T1 hypointensity (black holes), suggesting prior episodes of severe demyelination

findings.mass_effect: No mass effect or midline shift

findings.location: Left posterior periventricular white matter adjacent to the trigone of the left lateral ventricle
findings.borders: Open ring enhancement with the opening oriented toward the cortex, a pattern suggestive of demyelination rather than neoplasm or infection

findings.location: Right frontal and left parietal juxtacortical white matter

additional_observations: No definite Dawson fingers morphology — periventricular lesions are suggestive but not classic ovoid perpendicular orientation in all cases
Two T1 hypointense black holes suggesting prior episodes of demyelination
Brain parenchymal volume normal for age
No corpus callosum involvement identified on sagittal FLAIR
mri.impression: 1. Multiple white matter lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial locations, several of which are suggestive but not definitively classic for demyelinating disease. 2. Single enhancing lesion in the left posterior periventricular white matter with an open ring enhancement pattern favoring demyelination. 3. T1 hypointense black holes indicating prior inflammatory episodes. 4. The lesion distribution involves at least three of four typical MS regions (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial), though spinal cord has not yet been imaged. 5. While findings are concerning for multiple sclerosis, the absence of classic Dawson fingers and the borderline nature of some lesions warrants further evaluation including spinal cord imaging and CSF analysis to confirm the diagnosis.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial lesions with an enhancing lesion and T1 black holes; open ring enhancement favors demyelination
Periventricular lesions can occur in NMOSD but typically less numerous; would need AQP4 antibody testing and spinal cord imaging
Enhancing lesion and T1 black holes argue against this; migraine lesions are typically subcortical and non-enhancing
Enhancing lesions can occur but distribution and morphology less typical; would expect more heterogeneous involvement

recommended_actions: MRI of cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to assess for spinal cord lesions
Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
Aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
Visual evoked potentials to assess for subclinical optic nerve involvement

Inflammatory and Autoimmune Markers.clinical_significance: Low-titer positive ANA, nonspecific and of uncertain clinical significance; does not suggest systemic lupus
Mildly low despite supplementation; vitamin D deficiency has been associated with increased MS risk and disease activity

labs.interpretation: Routine laboratory studies are unremarkable with normal CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, thyroid function, and vitamin B12. ANA is low-titer positive (1:40 speckled) which is nonspecific and present in up to 15% of the general population. Vitamin D is mildly low at 28 ng/mL despite supplementation. Importantly, aquaporin-4 and MOG antibodies are both negative, making NMOSD and MOGAD unlikely. The overall laboratory profile does not reveal an alternative systemic or metabolic cause for the patient's neurological symptoms.
abnormal_values_summary: Vitamin D, 25-hydroxy: 29 ng/mL - Mildly low despite supplementation; vitamin D deficiency has been associated with increased MS risk and disease activity

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Present — 2 unique bands in CSF not found in paired serum sample

csf.interpretation: CSF analysis reveals normal cell count, normal protein and glucose. Oligoclonal bands are present but with only 2 unique bands in CSF (considered positive per McDonald criteria but at the lower end of specificity). IgG index is borderline elevated at 0.65. Mildly elevated myelin basic protein at 3.8 ng/mL is consistent with active demyelination but is not specific for MS. The CSF findings are supportive of but not definitively diagnostic for multiple sclerosis — the borderline nature of these results (only 2 OCBs, borderline IgG index) is consistent with a moderate-difficulty presentation that requires integration with clinical and imaging findings.

Visual Evoked Potentials.clinical_significance: Normal P100 latency in the right eye
Prolonged P100 latency indicating prior optic nerve demyelination, consistent with the patient's history of left optic neuritis 8 months ago
Mildly reduced amplitude in the left eye, consistent with partial axonal loss from prior optic neuritis
Significant interocular latency difference confirming asymmetric optic nerve conduction, consistent with prior unilateral demyelinating optic neuritis

output.interpretation: Visual evoked potentials demonstrate a prolonged P100 latency in the left eye (128 ms) with mildly reduced amplitude, and a significant interocular latency difference of 26 ms. The right eye responses are normal. These findings are consistent with prior demyelinating optic neuritis affecting the left optic nerve, corroborating the patient's clinical history of visual symptoms 8 months ago. This provides objective evidence of a prior demyelinating event in a location separate from the current clinical presentation, supporting dissemination in space.
abnormal_values_summary: P100 Latency - Left Eye: 128 ms ms - Prolonged P100 latency indicating prior optic nerve demyelination, consistent with the patient's history of left optic neuritis 8 months ago
P100 Amplitude - Left Eye: 4.8 uV uV - Mildly reduced amplitude in the left eye, consistent with partial axonal loss from prior optic neuritis
Interocular latency difference: 26 ms ms - Significant interocular latency difference confirming asymmetric optic nerve conduction, consistent with prior unilateral demyelinating optic neuritis

findings.location: C3-C4 level, dorsolateral spinal cord on the left
findings.size: Spanning approximately 1.5 vertebral segments in length, less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense lesion in the dorsolateral cord

findings.borders: Well-demarcated, peripheral location typical of demyelination

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense lesion in the posterior columns
signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Faint peripheral enhancement suggesting subacute activity

findings.mass_effect: Mild focal cord swelling at this level

additional_observations: Lesions are short-segment (each <2 vertebral segments), which favors MS over NMOSD where longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (>=3 segments) is typical
Cervical and thoracic disc spaces are preserved without significant degenerative changes
No extramedullary lesions or leptomeningeal enhancement
output.impression: 1. Two short-segment spinal cord lesions at C3-C4 and T8-T9, with imaging characteristics typical of demyelinating disease. 2. The thoracic lesion (T8-T9) demonstrates faint enhancement and mild cord swelling, suggesting subacute inflammatory activity that may correspond to the patient's progressive lower extremity weakness. 3. The cervical lesion (C3-C4) is non-enhancing, likely representing a prior subclinical event. 4. Short-segment morphology strongly favors MS over NMOSD. 5. Combined with brain MRI findings, the patient now has lesions disseminated in space across four typical MS regions (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) and evidence of dissemination in time (enhancing and non-enhancing lesions).

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Short-segment cord lesions in peripheral location with one enhancing and one non-enhancing lesion, combined with brain findings
Short-segment lesions (<2 vertebral segments each) argue against NMOSD; AQP4 antibody is negative

recommended_actions: Integrate spinal cord findings with brain MRI and CSF results to confirm McDonald criteria
Clinical correlation with sensory level and myelopathic signs

Optical Coherence Tomography.clinical_significance: Normal RNFL thickness in the right eye
Thinned RNFL in the left eye consistent with prior optic neuritis and axonal loss, particularly in the temporal quadrant
Significant temporal RNFL thinning, characteristic of prior optic neuritis from MS
Thinned ganglion cell layer consistent with retrograde neuronal degeneration from prior optic neuritis

output.interpretation: OCT demonstrates significant thinning of the retinal nerve fiber layer in the left eye (average 68 um, temporal quadrant 48 um) with corresponding ganglion cell layer thinning (61 um). The right eye is normal. These findings provide structural evidence of prior optic nerve damage in the left eye, consistent with the patient's reported episode of optic neuritis 8 months ago. The temporal quadrant thinning pattern is characteristic of demyelinating optic neuritis. This provides objective biomarker evidence of a prior inflammatory event affecting the left optic nerve.
abnormal_values_summary: RNFL Thickness - Left Eye (average): 68 um um - Thinned RNFL in the left eye consistent with prior optic neuritis and axonal loss, particularly in the temporal quadrant
RNFL Temporal Quadrant - Left Eye: 48 um um - Significant temporal RNFL thinning, characteristic of prior optic neuritis from MS
Ganglion Cell Layer - Left Eye: 61 um um - Thinned ganglion cell layer consistent with retrograde neuronal degeneration from prior optic neuritis

Extended Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Negative on highly sensitive cell-based assay, effectively excluding NMOSD

output.interpretation: Extended antibody testing including highly sensitive cell-based assays for aquaporin-4 and MOG antibodies are both negative, effectively excluding NMOSD and MOGAD from the differential diagnosis. Anti-GFAP antibody is negative. ACE level is normal, arguing against neurosarcoidosis. These results, combined with the clinical and imaging findings, further support the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis.

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular white matter — same lesions as prior study

signal_characteristics.T1: Previously noted T1 black holes unchanged

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: No enhancement (previously showed incomplete ring enhancement)

findings.location: Right periventricular white matter, anterior horn of the lateral ventricle

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, new compared to prior study

additional_observations: Previously identified juxtacortical and infratentorial lesions are stable
New enhancing lesion confirms ongoing disease activity despite interval clinical stability on treatment
output.impression: 1. New 6 mm enhancing lesion in the right periventricular white matter, not present on the prior study, indicating ongoing subclinical disease activity. 2. Resolution of enhancement in the previously active left posterior periventricular lesion, consistent with treatment effect. 3. Stable appearance of remaining lesion burden. 4. The development of a new enhancing lesion on follow-up imaging confirms dissemination in time and ongoing inflammatory activity. This may warrant reassessment of disease-modifying therapy efficacy.
recommended_actions: Discuss potential escalation of disease-modifying therapy given subclinical MRI activity
Repeat MRI in 6 months to monitor treatment response

output.query: multiple sclerosis relapsing-remitting first-line disease-modifying therapy guidelines 2024

results.title: Practice guideline recommendations: Disease-modifying therapies for adults with multiple sclerosis
results.authors: Rae-Grant A, Day GS, Marrie RA, et al.
results.key_finding: For RRMS, clinicians should offer disease-modifying therapy, with choice depending on patient factors including disease activity, comorbidities, and patient preferences. Options range from injectable therapies (interferon beta, glatiramer acetate) to oral agents (dimethyl fumarate, fingolimod, teriflunomide) to high-efficacy infusion therapies (natalizumab, ocrelizumab, alemtuzumab).

results.title: Early aggressive treatment versus escalation approach in relapsing-remitting MS
results.authors: Harding K, Williams O, Willis M, et al.
results.key_finding: Early high-efficacy therapy was associated with reduced long-term disability accumulation compared to initial treatment with moderate-efficacy agents followed by escalation.

results.title: Ocrelizumab versus Interferon Beta-1a in Relapsing Multiple Sclerosis (OPERA I and OPERA II)
results.authors: Hauser SL, Bar-Or A, Comi G, et al.
results.key_finding: Ocrelizumab reduced annualized relapse rates by 46-47% compared to interferon beta-1a, with significantly fewer new MRI lesions and reduced disability progression.

output.summary: Current evidence supports early initiation of disease-modifying therapy in relapsing-remitting MS. There is growing evidence favoring early high-efficacy therapy over a traditional escalation approach, particularly in patients with active disease (clinical relapses or MRI activity). First-line options include injectable therapies (interferon beta preparations, glatiramer acetate), oral agents (dimethyl fumarate, fingolimod, teriflunomide), and high-efficacy agents (natalizumab, ocrelizumab). For this patient with evidence of active disease (enhancing lesion, progressive symptoms), high-efficacy therapy such as ocrelizumab or natalizumab could be considered, though the choice must factor in JC virus antibody status, patient preferences, and reproductive planning.

interactions: Sertraline 50 mg: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Both can be used together safely.
warnings: GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain) are common, especially during initiation; may worsen existing IBS symptoms — consider starting at 120 mg BID for the first 7 days then increasing to 240 mg BID
Flushing is common; taking with food and aspirin 30 minutes prior may reduce flushing
Monitor lymphocyte count every 6 months — risk of lymphopenia; withhold if lymphocyte count <500 cells/uL for >6 months
Monitor CBC and LFTs at baseline, 6 months, and annually
Rare risk of PML in patients with prolonged lymphopenia
output.formulary_status: Preferred formulary agent for relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis
alternatives: Ocrelizumab 600 mg IV every 6 months (high-efficacy, consider if active disease warrants escalation)
Natalizumab 300 mg IV every 4 weeks (high-efficacy, requires JC virus antibody monitoring)
Glatiramer acetate 40 mg SC three times weekly (lower efficacy but favorable safety profile)
Teriflunomide 14 mg daily (oral, requires hepatic monitoring, teratogenic)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Multiple sclerosis, relapsing-remitting type, with subacute myelopathy as presenting clinical event and evidence of prior subclinical demyelinating episodes

differential.key_distinguishing: AQP4 antibodies negative, short-segment spinal cord lesions (<2 vertebral segments), periventricular and juxtacortical brain lesions present — all favoring MS over NMOSD
MOG antibodies negative, T1 black holes and periventricular lesion distribution more typical of MS; MOGAD typically has better recovery and bilateral optic neuritis
Normal ESR/CRP, open ring enhancement pattern typical of demyelination, periventricular lesion distribution characteristic of MS rather than vasculitis
No leptomeningeal enhancement, normal ACE level, no pulmonary symptoms, lesion morphology not typical for sarcoid
Enhancing lesion, T1 black holes, spinal cord involvement, CSF oligoclonal bands, and progressive neurological deficits all exclude benign migraine-related white matter changes

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed neurological history including temporal profile of symptoms, prior episodes (even transient and resolved), and triggers such as heat sensitivity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identifies at least two prior episodes: left optic neuritis 8 months ago and right arm/forearm paresthesias 14 months ago, plus current progressive myelopathy. Uhthoff phenomenon present.

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including cranial nerve assessment with visual acuity, fundoscopy, and RAPD testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left eye visual acuity reduced, RAPD present on left, bilateral gaze-evoked nystagmus, left-predominant lower extremity weakness, positive Babinski on left, Lhermitte sign positive

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple white matter lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial locations with one enhancing lesion showing open ring pattern

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and TSH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal except mildly low vitamin D; low-titer ANA nonspecific; no alternative systemic diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Order aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Both negative, excluding NMOSD and MOGAD

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cell count, protein, and glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Oligoclonal bands present (2 bands), borderline IgG index, mildly elevated myelin basic protein — supportive but borderline findings

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to evaluate for spinal cord lesions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Two short-segment spinal cord lesions at C3-C4 and T8-T9, with thoracic lesion showing enhancement corresponding to current symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to objectively document prior optic nerve demyelination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prolonged P100 latency in the left eye confirming prior optic neuritis

optimal_actions.action: Apply 2017 McDonald diagnostic criteria to integrate clinical, MRI, and CSF findings to confirm diagnosis of MS
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Criteria met: DIS (periventricular + juxtacortical + infratentorial + spinal cord) and DIT (enhancing + non-enhancing lesions, plus prior clinical episodes). CSF OCBs provide additional supportive evidence.

optimal_actions.action: Treat acute relapse with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3-5 days
Initiate disease-modifying therapy for relapsing-remitting MS, discussing options including moderate-efficacy and high-efficacy agents based on disease activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Selection of appropriate DMT based on disease activity, patient preferences, and safety profile

optimal_actions.action: Arrange follow-up MRI in 3 months to establish new baseline and monitor for treatment response, with clinical follow-up in neurology

critical_actions: Identify prior clinical episodes (optic neuritis, arm paresthesias) that suggest dissemination in time — these were not initially recognized as demyelinating events
Order brain and spinal cord MRI with contrast to demonstrate dissemination in space across multiple CNS regions
Perform lumbar puncture for CSF oligoclonal bands, which can substitute for clinical dissemination in time under 2017 McDonald criteria
Exclude NMOSD and MOGAD with aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing before committing to an MS diagnosis
Apply 2017 McDonald diagnostic criteria systematically to confirm the diagnosis
Initiate acute relapse treatment with IV corticosteroids
Initiate disease-modifying therapy to prevent future relapses and disability accumulation
contraindicated_actions: Attributing symptoms to functional neurological disorder without completing a thorough workup including MRI and CSF analysis
Diagnosing MS based solely on MRI white matter lesions without meeting McDonald criteria and excluding mimics
Starting natalizumab without first checking JC virus antibody status
Starting teriflunomide without pregnancy testing and contraception counseling in a woman of childbearing age
Failing to image the spinal cord when myelopathic signs are present
Treating with chronic oral corticosteroids rather than pulse IV steroids for acute relapse — chronic steroids are ineffective and harmful in MS
key_reasoning_points: The patient has three separate clinical episodes affecting different CNS locations (optic neuritis, hand/forearm paresthesias, myelopathy) — this establishes dissemination in time clinically, even though the first two were not initially recognized as demyelinating events

Brain MRI shows lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial regions, but the findings are not classic textbook MS (no definite Dawson fingers, borderline lesion characteristics) — requiring spinal cord imaging to complete the DIS assessment

CSF findings are supportive but borderline (only 2 OCBs, borderline IgG index), reflecting the moderate difficulty — an agent must integrate imperfect data across multiple modalities rather than relying on any single test
The open ring enhancement pattern on MRI strongly favors demyelination over tumor or infection — this is a key imaging discriminator
Short-segment spinal cord lesions (<2 vertebral segments) favor MS over NMOSD, where longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (>=3 segments) is typical
Lhermitte sign and Uhthoff phenomenon are clinical features highly suggestive of MS that should raise diagnostic suspicion even before imaging
Negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies are essential to exclude MS mimics before initiating disease-modifying therapy
Low vitamin D is both a risk factor for MS and a modifiable comorbidity that should be addressed as part of comprehensive management

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate — first clinical presentation with subacute myelopathy but prior subclinical episodes not yet recognized as MS. MRI suggestive but not classic; CSF borderline positive. Requires integration of clinical history, imaging, and laboratory data to meet diagnostic criteria.
clinical_pearls: Uhthoff phenomenon (heat-induced worsening) is nearly pathognomonic for demyelinating disease and should trigger MS evaluation
Lhermitte sign (electric sensation with neck flexion) localizes to the cervical spinal cord and is highly associated with MS
Open ring enhancement on MRI has high specificity for demyelinating lesions versus tumor or infection — the opening faces the cortex (gray matter)

The 2017 McDonald criteria allow CSF oligoclonal bands to substitute for clinical dissemination in time, which is critical for diagnosing MS after a single clinical event (clinically isolated syndrome) with MRI evidence of prior subclinical lesions
Short-segment spinal cord lesions (<2 vertebral segments) favor MS, while longitudinally extensive lesions (>=3 segments) should raise concern for NMOSD
